claim: PET beverage bottle collection-for-recycling rates in Vietnam are already high — an estimated 45-65% nationally, with 62% in Ho Chi Minh City specifically — meaning most PET bottles in HCMC are already being captured by existing collection, which weakens the case that a hobby robot adds new PET recovery value.
quote: The recycling rate for PET beverage bottles is estimated to be 45 to 65 percent. ... Previous research by the study team found the CFR rate for PET bottles to be 62 percent in Ho Chi Minh City and 39 percent in Hanoi.

claim: Roughly 90% of collected PET bottles in Vietnam are handled by the informal sector (ve chai), and Ho Chi Minh City's informal collector workforce is about 6,200 people (2,000 street material pickers plus 4,200 independent waste pickers) — any robotic sorting proposition competes with or feeds this existing manual value chain.
quote: According to an estimate, 90 percent of these PET bottles, collected for recycling, are handled by the informal sector. ... the informal waste collector workforce comprises 6,200 people, i.e., 2,000 street material pickers and 4,200 independent waste pickers.

claim: HDPE and PP caps and sleeves on PET bottles are treated as contamination that is already removed downstream during recyclers' sorting and cleaning processes — cap separation from bottles is an existing step in the value chain, not an unmet need at the collection stage.
quote: the CFR rate of PET is determined after contaminants like dirt metals and other plastics (sleeves and caps from other materials such as HDPE and PP) have been removed.

claim: Informal collection volumes in Vietnam are price-elastic: when recycled resin prices (rPET, rPP, rHDPE) drop, informal collectors and aggregators slow or stop collecting because it becomes unprofitable — so the economic viability of any sorting operation depends on volatile recycled-resin prices, not fixed per-kg values.
quote: As rPET prices drop, these stakeholders slow down plastic waste collection and aggregation because recycling becomes less profitable. When rPET prices rise, the collection increases again as these stakeholders take advantage of the higher prices.

claim: Vietnam recycles only about 33% of PET/PP/HDPE/LDPE with a 77% value yield, unlocking just 25% of potential material value (US$872M/yr) and losing US$2.2-2.9 billion per year; CFR rates for PP and HDPE are only 20-50%, with easily separable rigid items (e.g., caps, shampoo bottles) at the high end and films/flexibles at the low end — the recovery gap is concentrated in films and contaminated flexibles, not rigid bottles and caps.
quote: Currently, only 25 percent of the total material value of plastics, or US$872 million per year is unlocked, based on a 33 percent recycling rate and 77 percent value yield from plastic recycling. This results in the loss of US$2.2 billion to US$2.9 billion per year ... The CFR rate for HDPE is estimated to be 20 to 50 percent. ... The recycling rate for easily separable rigid HDPE (e.g., for shampoo containers) is estimated to be at the higher end while for other HDPE applications (e.g., films, plastic bags, and so forth), it is estimated to be much lower.

claim: Industrial recyclers in Vietnam separate PET bottle material from cap material using density (float/sink) in water tanks, because PET flakes sink while HDPE cap flakes float — meaning a hobby robot does not need to identify cap resin optically; a water bath after shredding (or simple float test) is the established cheap separation method for PET-vs-HDPE/PP.
quote: Separation of different types of plastic flakes in large water tank Some plastics float and some sink (e.g. caps attached to bottles are made of different plastics (e.g. PET bottle flakes (sinks) vs HDPE caps flakes (float)).

claim: The separation that adds the most value in the Vietnam PET chain is clear+clean PET vs colored/opaque PET: high-quality rPET made from clear, clean PET bottles sells for about VND 25,000–30,000/kg, so a sorter that isolates clear PET (a color/transparency task feasible with RGB cameras) targets the highest-value output stream.
quote: rPET produced to higher quality requires clean, high-quality feedstock (clear and clean PET plastic bottles). Higher-quality rPET can be sold for about VND25,000–VND30,000/kilogram (kg). ... Clear PET has greater value than colored or opaque PET, which can disrupt the recycling process.

claim: The informal sector (ve chai) collects roughly half of Vietnam's PET packaging waste, and in Ho Chi Minh City the collected-for-recycling rate for PET bottles is 62% with about 90% of those bottles handled by the informal sector — so a hobby sorting arm would sit inside an already-efficient informal collection chain rather than filling a collection gap.
quote: The informal sector is estimated to collect about 50% of PET plastic packaging waste, with a higher share for PET bottles in large cities. ... Reported CFRs for PET bottles were 62% in Ho Chi Minh City and in 39% in Ha Noi, with about 90% of these bottles handled by the informal sector.

claim: Commercial material recovery operations sort recyclables into aluminium, paper, and plastic types (PET vs HDPE vs PP) using optical sorters programmed to detect a specific resin — the industrial baseline a camera-only hobby arm would be approximating.
quote: Sort through all the recyclables into categories: aluminium, paper, and different types of plastic (i.e. PET, HDPE and PP, using optical sorters).

claim: Vietnam's PET packaging waste generation in 2024 is estimated at 487,714 tons, indicating a large feedstock base for any downstream sorting/processing improvement.
quote: PET packaging waste generation in 2024 is estimated at 487,714 tons.

claim: A Pi0.5 flow-matching policy trained on only 100 teleoperated demonstrations on an SO-101 Pro achieved 87-93% success (13/15 clean grasp-and-lift, 1 partial, 1 positional miss) on a single red-bottle pick task with no simulation or domain randomization.
quote: Counting the one grasped-but-incomplete trial as partial successes, the effective success rate is 87–93%. For a first policy trained on 100 episodes with no domain randomization, no simulation pre-training, and a low-cost arm, this is an encouraging baseline.

claim: The SO-101's STS3215 servos exhibit up to 7 degrees of joint tracking error under load, and raising PID gains to fix it induces arm shaking that corrupts the wrist-camera image at exactly the pre-grasp moment — a hardware ceiling on grasp precision independent of the learned policy.
quote: The first three joints exhibited tracking errors of up to 7 degrees under the current PID values. Increasing the P gain for various D values reduced tracking error but introduced arm shaking

claim: The reported success rate holds only inside a narrow operational envelope (one object, one approximate position range, controlled lighting, restricted servo angles); moving the bottle outside that envelope sharply degrades performance and multi-object behavior is untested.
quote: Move the bottle outside that servos envelope and the number drops sharply. Add a second object and the policy has not been tested at all.

claim: SmolVLA fine-tuned on the identical 100-episode dataset performed significantly worse than Pi0.5 and failed to produce reliable grasps, suggesting 100 episodes is insufficient for smaller-backbone VLA models to bootstrap manipulation on this hardware.
quote: Performance was significantly worse than Pi0.5 and the approach did not produce reliable grasps... 100 episodes may simply be insufficient for SmolVLA to bootstrap reliable manipulation behavior on this task.

claim: Recovery demonstrations that start from failure states degraded policy performance; corrections work only when embedded as small lateral adjustments inside otherwise complete successful trajectories, and a wrist camera cannot be substituted by software foveal crops from fixed overhead cameras.
quote: An early attempt at collecting recovery data by starting episodes directly from failed arm positions made performance worse... The foveal crops lacked the stable egocentric reference frame that the wrist camera provides
